title: Myasthenia Gravis
oneLiner: Autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder causing fluctuating, fatigable weakness, often ocular/bulbar; treatable but can lead to myasthenic crisis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 35-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of **intermittent double vision** and **drooping of the right eyelid**, worse at the end of the day. She also notices that her speech becomes slurred after talking for a few minutes, and she has trouble chewing tough foods. On examination, you find **proximal limb weakness** (shoulder abduction, hip flexion) that worsens with sustained effort (e.g., looking up for 30 seconds). There is no sensory loss, reflexes are normal, and there is no pain.

**Pattern Clues & What They Tell You:**

- **Fluctuating, fatigable weakness** – hallmark of MG; weakness gets worse with use, better with rest.  
- **Ocular onset (ptosis, diplopia)** – 50-60% present with eye symptoms; virtually all develop ocular involvement eventually.  
- **Bulbar symptoms (dysarthria, dysphagia, chewing fatigue)** – indicates involvement of cranial nerves IX, X, XII; high risk for aspiration.  
- **Proximal > distal limb weakness** – typical for neuromuscular junction disorders; distal sparing helps differentiate from myopathy.  
- **No sensory loss, normal reflexes** – pure motor; sensory or reflex changes point to neuropathic or myelopathic causes.  
- **Worse at day’s end** – diurnal variation; muscle use accumulates neurotransmitter depletion.  
- **Young adult female** – peak incidence 20-40 years; women > men at onset under 50.

**Atypical Presentations (the tricksters):**

1. **Pure ocular MG** – only ptosis and diplopia, no limb/bulbar for >2 years. Can mimic third nerve palsy or internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Look for **fatigability** (e.g., sustained upgaze worsens ptosis).  
2. **Elderly male with isolated bulbar weakness** – mistaken for stroke or motor neuron disease. Key clue: **fluctuation** – symptoms improve after a nap.  
3. **Post-vaccination or post-infection exacerbation** – rapid onset of weakness, often in new diagnosis. Can be confused with Guillain-Barré (but no areflexia, no sensory loss).

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an **autoimmune disease** where antibodies attack the **acetylcholine receptors** (or related proteins) at the neuromuscular junction. This reduces the number of functioning receptors, so the signal from nerve to muscle is weaker, especially with repeated use. The result is **fatigable muscle weakness** that fluctuates over minutes to days.

**Why it matters:**  

- **Prevalence:** ~20/100,000 – not rare; you will see it.  
- **Treatable:** With immunosuppression and anticholinesterase meds, most patients achieve good control.  
- **Life-threatening:** Myasthenic crisis with respiratory failure requires ICU support.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosed as stroke, MS, or psychogenic weakness. A missed diagnosis leads to unnecessary investigations and delayed treatment.  
- **Strongly associated with thymoma** – every MG patient needs chest imaging.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:**  
The normal neuromuscular junction: an action potential at the nerve terminal releases acetylcholine (ACh) into the synaptic cleft. ACh binds to receptors on the muscle endplate, causing depolarization and muscle contraction.  

In MG, the immune system produces **IgG antibodies** against the **nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (AChR)** in **85%** of cases. Some have antibodies to **muscle-specific kinase (MuSK)** or **LRP4**. These antibodies:  

- Block ACh binding (competitive)  
- Accelerate receptor endocytosis and degradation (downregulation)  
- Fix complement, damaging the postsynaptic membrane  

The result: **reduced number of functional ACh receptors**. With each nerve impulse, fewer receptors are available to depolarise the muscle. **Repetitive stimulation** (muscle use) depletes the already limited ACh vesicles, and the weakened postsynaptic membrane cannot sustain the signal → **fatigable weakness**.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Sign |
|-----------|------|
| Reduced ACh receptors at neuromuscular junction | **Generalised weakness** with use |
| Extraocular muscles are particularly sensitive (high firing rate, low safety factor) | **Ptosis, diplopia** – earliest and most common |
| Bulbar muscles (pharynx, tongue, cheek) are fatigable | **Dysarthria**, **dysphagia**, **jaw drop** |
| Proximal limb muscles have larger motor units and lower safety margin | **Proximal weakness** (shoulder abductors, hip flexors) |
| Depletion of ACh release with sustained contraction | **Fatigability on repeated testing** – e.g., upgaze for 30s worsens ptosis |
| Complement-mediated damage disrupts postsynaptic folds | **Irreversible changes** in chronic untreated MG – but still responsive to immunosuppression |

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition:**  
The fluctuating weakness is not “all-or-nothing” but depends on **use**. So the patient's history of “double vision at the end of the workday” or “can’t chew steak anymore” directly reflects the **depletion of effective ACh receptors**. The diurnal variation is classic. The absence of pain excludes inflammation (myositis). Normal reflexes prove the nerve and muscle are intact; the defect is in the **transmission** between them.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**Most common cause:** **Autoimmune (idiopathic)** – 80-90% of cases. But there are important secondary triggers and associations.

| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| **Autoimmune** | AChR antibody-positive MG (85%) | “Fatigable weakness + positive antibody” |
| **Autoimmune** | MuSK antibody-positive MG (5-10%) | “Bulbar-predominant, ptosis but no diplopia, often female” |
| **Thymic pathology** | Thymoma (~15% of MG patients) | “Any MG patient – think thymoma; older age, faster onset” |
| **Thymic pathology** | Thymic hyperplasia (younger patients) | “Under 40, female, anti-AChR positive” |
| **Drug-induced** | Penicillamine, statins, aminoglycosides, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, magnesium, curare-like agents | “MG first presentation after starting a new drug” |
| **Paraneoplastic** | Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) – see differentials | “Proximal weakness that improves with use, autonomic symptoms, small cell lung cancer” |
| **Infection / stress** | Any infection (especially URTI), surgery, pregnancy | “Worsening of known MG or first crisis after a febrile illness” |
| **Genetic (rare)** | Congenital myasthenic syndromes (CMS) | “Onset at birth or childhood, no antibodies, no fluctuation? think CMS” |

**Key takeaway:** Always check for **thymoma** and ask about drug history. One dose of an aminoglycoside can unmask MG.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (simplified):**  
Diagnosis rests on:  

1. **Clinical** – fluctuating, fatigable weakness (especially ocular/bulbar).  
2. **Antibody testing** – anti-AChR or anti-MuSK.  
3. **Electrophysiology** – repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) or single-fibre EMG (SFEMG).  
4. **Edrophonium (Tensilon) test** – now rarely used due to safety; still occasionally done if equivocal.

**Tests in detail:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Anti-AChR antibodies** | Binding antibodies to nicotinic AChR | First-line serology; positive in ~85% of generalised MG | **Positive confirms autoimmune MG** (but 10-15% of ocular MG are negative). Titer does not correlate with severity. |
| **Anti-MuSK antibodies** | Antibodies to muscle-specific kinase | If AChR-negative but high clinical suspicion (especially bulbar) | Positive = MuSK-MG (often more severe, prednisolone less effective; needs rituximab/plasma exchange). |
| **CT chest with contrast** | Thymoma / thymic hyperplasia | **Mandatory in all new MG** – 15% have thymoma | Thymoma = surgical resection. Hyperplasia may prompt thymectomy in young AChR+ patients. |
| **Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS)** | Decremental response (>10% drop in amplitude at low-frequency stimulation) | Objective evidence of neuromuscular transmission defect | **Positive confirms MG** – high specificity. False negatives possible if only ocular. |
| **Single-fibre EMG** | Increased jitter (variability in time between two muscle fibres) | Most sensitive test – even picks up ocular MG | **Highly sensitive >95%.** Can be positive in other NMJ disorders (LEMS, botulism) so not specific alone. |
| **Edrophonium test** (if done) | Rapid (<1 min) improvement in strength after IV edrophonium | Quick bedside confirmation (rarely used now) | **Improvement strongly suggests MG.** Risk of bradycardia – have atropine ready. |

**Single finding that confirms diagnosis:**  
A **positive anti-AChR antibody** in a patient with compatible clinical features.

**Single finding that rules out MG (almost):**  
A **normal single-fibre EMG** from a clinically weak muscle (e.g., frontalis, extensor digitorum) – but still 5% false negatives.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – for new diagnosis or crisis:**

- **Assess airway, breathing, circulation.** If respiratory distress (tachypnoea, accessory muscles, unable to count to 20 in one breath, or dropping O2 sats) → **call ICU** – myasthenic crisis.  
- **NIF (negative inspiratory force) < -20 cmH2O or forced vital capacity < 15 mL/kg** – immediate intubation indication.  
- **Stop any neuromuscular blocking agents (NMBs)** – e.g., vecuronium, rocuronium (prolonged effect in MG).  
- **Administer pyridostigmine** (if already on it) – but in crisis, IV neostigmine may be used first by anaesthetist (rare).  

**Ward (first 24-72 hours) – stabilisation and workup:**

- **Start pyridostigmine** 30-60 mg PO 3-4 times daily (max 120 mg/dose). CR if needed. *Why:* anticholinesterase increases ACh availability at synaptic cleft. Onset 30-60 min, duration 3-4 hours.  
- **Corticosteroids** – **prednisolone** 20-40 mg daily (or start 10 mg and taper up to avoid early exacerbation). *Why:* immunosuppression – turns off antibody production. **Watch for initial worsening** in first 2 weeks (steroid-induced exacerbation). Have rescue plan (IVIG/plasma exchange).  
- **Azathioprine** (1-2 mg/kg/day) or mycophenolate mofetil – as steroid-sparing. Takes 4-6 months to work.  
- **IVIG** (0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days) or **plasma exchange** (5-7 exchanges over 2 weeks) – for acute severe exacerbations or crisis.  
- **Thymectomy** – indicated if thymoma (any age) or if AChR+ generalised MG < 60 years (improves outcomes). Wait until stable, often after steroids are established.  

**Long-term:**

- **Maintenance immunosuppression** – prednisolone weaned to lowest effective dose (often 5-10 mg daily) plus azathioprine/mycophenolate.  
- **Pyridostigmine** as needed for symptoms.  
- **Rituximab** – for refractory MG (especially MuSK+).  
- **Avoid** – statins, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, beta-blockers (including eye drops for glaucoma), verapamil, magnesium.  

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do NOT give magnesium** – it blocks ACh release and can precipitate crisis.  
- **Do NOT use suxamethonium** for anaesthesia – metabolism is normal, but depolarising block can be prolonged if receptors are damaged? Actually, suxamethonium often *resistance* in MG? Wait: MG patients may be resistant to depolarising NMB (suxamethonium) and sensitive to non-depolarising (rocuronium). But safer to avoid both or use at reduced doses.  
- **Do NOT start high-dose prednisolone without planning for exacerbation** – start low and build.  
- **Do NOT forget to check thymoma** – missing a tumour.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Myasthenia Gravis | Lambert-Eaton (LEMS) | Botulism | Oculopharyngeal MD | ALS (Bulbar) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Pain** | None | None | None (blurred vision, but no pain) | None | None (but cramps) |
| **Exam** | Fatigable weakness, normal reflexes, no sensory loss | Proximal weakness *improves* with exercise, depressed/absent reflexes, autonomic (dry mouth, impotence) | Descending flaccid paralysis, dilated fixed pupils, bulbar, preserved EOM? In foodborne, symmetric, no fever | Slowly progressive ptosis, dysphagia, no fluctuation; family history | Spastic + flaccid, fasciculations, hyperreflexia, dysarthria, dysphagia with emotional lability |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | Anti-AChR/MuSK antibodies | Anti-VGCC antibodies (P/Q-type) | Stool/serum botulinum toxin | Genetic test (PABPN1 expansion) | EMG shows denervation, normal NMJ studies |
| **Key diff** | Fatigability, fluctuation, response to pyridostigmine | **Improvement with exercise**; associated with small cell lung cancer | **Acute onset**, history of contaminated food, autonomic signs (dry mouth, constipation) | **Family history**, gradual onset, no fluctuation | **UMN+LMN signs** (spasticity, fasciculations, brisk reflexes), relentless progression |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complication: Myasthenic crisis**

- **Presentation:** Rapid worsening of weakness, especially bulbar and respiratory. Patient can’t hold head up, can’t swallow saliva, becomes tachypnoeic, uses accessory muscles, eventually *silent chest* with desaturation.  
- **Key signs:** Staccato speech (short, soft phrases), nasal regurgitation, inability to lift head from pillow (*head drop* – very specific).  
- **What to do:** **Call ICU immediately.** Measure NIF and FVC. If FVC < 15 mL/kg or NIF < -20 cmH2O → intubate elective (before crash). Start IVIG or plasma exchange. **Do not give sedatives** – they may precipitate respiratory arrest.  
- **Other complications:**  
  - **Aspiration pneumonia** due to dysphagia  
  - **Thymoma** – risk of invasion, but thymic carcinoma is rarer.  
  - **Immunosuppression side effects**: osteoporosis, infections, diabetes.

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**  
- Patient with “long-standing ptosis and double vision” who eventually presents in crisis after a chest infection.  
- Elderly man with swallowing difficulty and weight loss, misdiagnosed as oesophageal cancer, but actually MG with bulbar onset.  
- Post-operative myasthenic crisis after a minor procedure (especially if non-depolarising NMBs were used).

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **ICE PACK test** – *Not a mnemonic, but a pearl:* Place an ice pack over a ptotic eyelid for 2 minutes. **If ptosis improves** → MG (cold slows acetylcholinesterase, increasing ACh at synapse).  
2. **MG’s Don’ts** – Remember: **Statins, Aminoglycosides, Macrolides, Fluoroquinolones, Beta-blockers, Magnesium**. Mnemonic: “**S.A.M. FlaM**” (Statins, Aminoglycosides, Macrolides, Fluoroquinolones, Magnesium). Beta-blockers added separately.  
3. **FOCAL weakness** – F: Fatigable, O: Ocular (ptosis/diplopia), C: Cranial/ bulbar, A: Antibodies (AChR/MuSK), L: LEMS mimics? Not perfect but helps recall features.

**Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

- **“Ice pack test”** is highly specific for MG ptosis – improvement suggests MG.  
- **“Peek sign”** – when you ask the patient to look up and close eyes tightly, ptosis worsens.  
- **“Lid twitch”** – after upgaze, when patient looks down, the eyelid twitches transiently (Cogan’s lid twitch).  
- **“Cogwheel” eye movements** – hypometric saccades with overshoot.  
- **Observe patient drinking water** – if they must pause to swallow or water dribbles from mouth, think bulbar MG.  
- **AChR antibodies can be negative in 15%**, especially ocular – if suspicion high, proceed to EMG.  
- **Thymoma on CT** – even if asymptomatic, resect because of malignant potential.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking MG for stroke – bilateral ptosis and diplopia do not fit single stroke lesion.  
- Giving edrophonium without atropine at bedside – bradycardia can be severe.  
- Prescribing antibiotics for a chest infection without checking safety – avoid macrolides and fluoroquinolones.  
- Starting steroids without first evaluating for thymoma – steroids can shrink hyperplasia but mask a thymoma.  

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

- **Respiratory failure** is the priority. Escalate if:
  - **FVC < 15 mL/kg** (or < 1 L in average adult)  
  - **NIF (negative inspiratory force) < -20 cmH2O**  
  - **SpO2 < 92% on room air** or dropping despite O2  
  - **Inability to clear secretions** (bulbar weakness)  
  - **Staccato speech** (can’t count to 20 in one breath)  
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (or ≥ 3 in any single parameter – desaturation, tachypnoea, tachycardia)  

- **Also escalate if:**  
  - **New onset of bulbar symptoms** (dysphagia to solids or liquids) – risk of aspiration  
  - **Rapidly worsening weakness over hours** – consider myasthenic crisis  
  - **Signs of cholinergic crisis** (if on pyridostigmine overdose) – muscle fasciculations, increased salivation, diarrhoea, bradycardia. Paradoxically, weakness may worsen. This is a **medical emergency** – stop pyridostigmine, call senior.

- **ICU admission criteria:**  
  - Any of the above respiratory signs  
  - Need for non-invasive ventilation (NIV) or intubation  
  - Severe dysphagia with inability to handle secretions (consider NG tube and NBM)

**Specific thresholds:**

| Parameter | Escalation Trigger |
|-----------|-------------------|
| FVC | < 15 mL/kg (adult ~ 1-1.2 L) |
| NIF | < -20 cmH2O (or > -20 indicates inadequate effort) |
| NEWS2 | ≥ 5 (or single parameter ≥ 3) |
| GCS | < 15 (if due to hypercapnia or sedation – avoid in MG) |
| Lactate | Not specific to MG, but rising lactate may indicate sepsis or ischemia |

**Remember:** In myasthenic crisis, intubate **early** before exhaustion. Prolonged bag-mask ventilation is difficult due to weak muscles. Have a low threshold for ICU.

---

*End of STUDY-MODE entry. You now have a dense, conceptual map of Myasthenia Gravis – pattern, mechanism, workup, management, and danger zones. Use it to build your own mental model.*

## Myasthenia Gravis  
**One-liner:** Autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder causing fluctuating, fatigable weakness, often ocular/bulbar; treatable but can lead to myasthenic crisis.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See  

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 35-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of **intermittent double vision** and **drooping of the right eyelid**, worse at the end of the day. She also notices that her speech becomes slurred after talking for a few minutes, and she has trouble chewing tough foods. On examination, you find **proximal limb weakness** (shoulder abduction, hip flexion) that worsens with sustained effort (e.g., looking up for 30 seconds). There is no sensory loss, reflexes are normal, and there is no pain.

**Pattern Clues & What They Tell You:**

- **Fluctuating, fatigable weakness** – hallmark of MG; weakness gets worse with use, better with rest.  
- **Ocular onset (ptosis, diplopia)** – 50-60% present with eye symptoms; virtually all develop ocular involvement eventually.  
- **Bulbar symptoms (dysarthria, dysphagia, chewing fatigue)** – indicates involvement of cranial nerves IX, X, XII; high risk for aspiration.  
- **Proximal > distal limb weakness** – typical for neuromuscular junction disorders; distal sparing helps differentiate from myopathy.  
- **No sensory loss, normal reflexes** – pure motor; sensory or reflex changes point to neuropathic or myelopathic causes.  
- **Worse at day’s end** – diurnal variation; muscle use accumulates neurotransmitter depletion.  
- **Young adult female** – peak incidence 20-40 years; women > men at onset under 50.

**Atypical Presentations (the tricksters):**

1. **Pure ocular MG** – only ptosis and diplopia, no limb/bulbar for >2 years. Can mimic third nerve palsy or internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Look for **fatigability** (e.g., sustained upgaze worsens ptosis).  
2. **Elderly male with isolated bulbar weakness** – mistaken for stroke or motor neuron disease. Key clue: **fluctuation** – symptoms improve after a nap.  
3. **Post-vaccination or post-infection exacerbation** – rapid onset of weakness, often in new diagnosis. Can be confused with Guillain-Barré (but no areflexia, no sensory loss).

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an **autoimmune disease** where antibodies attack the **acetylcholine receptors** (or related proteins) at the neuromuscular junction. This reduces the number of functioning receptors, so the signal from nerve to muscle is weaker, especially with repeated use. The result is **fatigable muscle weakness** that fluctuates over minutes to days.

**Why it matters:**  

- **Prevalence:** ~20/100,000 – not rare; you will see it.  
- **Treatable:** With immunosuppression and anticholinesterase meds, most patients achieve good control.  
- **Life-threatening:** Myasthenic crisis with respiratory failure requires ICU support.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosed as stroke, MS, or psychogenic weakness. A missed diagnosis leads to unnecessary investigations and delayed treatment.  
- **Strongly associated with thymoma** – every MG patient needs chest imaging.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:**  
The normal neuromuscular junction: an action potential at the nerve terminal releases acetylcholine (ACh) into the synaptic cleft. ACh binds to receptors on the muscle endplate, causing depolarization and muscle contraction.  

In MG, the immune system produces **IgG antibodies** against the **nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (AChR)** in **85%** of cases. Some have antibodies to **muscle-specific kinase (MuSK)** or **LRP4**. These antibodies:  

- Block ACh binding (competitive)  
- Accelerate receptor endocytosis and degradation (downregulation)  
- Fix complement, damaging the postsynaptic membrane  

The result: **reduced number of functional ACh receptors**. With each nerve impulse, fewer receptors are available to depolarise the muscle. **Repetitive stimulation** (muscle use) depletes the already limited ACh vesicles, and the weakened postsynaptic membrane cannot sustain the signal → **fatigable weakness**.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Sign |
|-----------|------|
| Reduced ACh receptors at neuromuscular junction | **Generalised weakness** with use |
| Extraocular muscles are particularly sensitive (high firing rate, low safety factor) | **Ptosis, diplopia** – earliest and most common |
| Bulbar muscles (pharynx, tongue, cheek) are fatigable | **Dysarthria**, **dysphagia**, **jaw drop** |
| Proximal limb muscles have larger motor units and lower safety margin | **Proximal weakness** (shoulder abductors, hip flexors) |
| Depletion of ACh release with sustained contraction | **Fatigability on repeated testing** – e.g., upgaze for 30s worsens ptosis |
| Complement-mediated damage disrupts postsynaptic folds | **Irreversible changes** in chronic untreated MG – but still responsive to immunosuppression |

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition:**  
The fluctuating weakness is not “all-or-nothing” but depends on **use**. So the patient's history of “double vision at the end of the workday” or “can’t chew steak anymore” directly reflects the **depletion of effective ACh receptors**. The diurnal variation is classic. The absence of pain excludes inflammation (myositis). Normal reflexes prove the nerve and muscle are intact; the defect is in the **transmission** between them.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

**Most common cause:** **Autoimmune (idiopathic)** – 80-90% of cases. But there are important secondary triggers and associations.

| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| **Autoimmune** | AChR antibody-positive MG (85%) | “Fatigable weakness + positive antibody” |
| **Autoimmune** | MuSK antibody-positive MG (5-10%) | “Bulbar-predominant, ptosis but no diplopia, often female” |
| **Thymic pathology** | Thymoma (~15% of MG patients) | “Any MG patient – think thymoma; older age, faster onset” |
| **Thymic pathology** | Thymic hyperplasia (younger patients) | “Under 40, female, anti-AChR positive” |
| **Drug-induced** | Penicillamine, statins, aminoglycosides, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, magnesium, curare-like agents | “MG first presentation after starting a new drug” |
| **Paraneoplastic** | Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) – see differentials | “Proximal weakness that improves with use, autonomic symptoms, small cell lung cancer” |
| **Infection / stress** | Any infection (especially URTI), surgery, pregnancy | “Worsening of known MG or first crisis after a febrile illness” |
| **Genetic (rare)** | Congenital myasthenic syndromes (CMS) | “Onset at birth or childhood, no antibodies, no fluctuation? think CMS” |

**Key takeaway:** Always check for **thymoma** and ask about drug history. One dose of an aminoglycoside can unmask MG.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (simplified):**  
Diagnosis rests on:  

1. **Clinical** – fluctuating, fatigable weakness (especially ocular/bulbar).  
2. **Antibody testing** – anti-AChR or anti-MuSK.  
3. **Electrophysiology** – repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) or single-fibre EMG (SFEMG).  
4. **Edrophonium (Tensilon) test** – now rarely used due to safety; still occasionally done if equivocal.

**Tests in detail:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Anti-AChR antibodies** | Binding antibodies to nicotinic AChR | First-line serology; positive in ~85% of generalised MG | **Positive confirms autoimmune MG** (but 10-15% of ocular MG are negative). Titer does not correlate with severity. |
| **Anti-MuSK antibodies** | Antibodies to muscle-specific kinase | If AChR-negative but high clinical suspicion (especially bulbar) | Positive = MuSK-MG (often more severe, prednisolone less effective; needs rituximab/plasma exchange). |
| **CT chest with contrast** | Thymoma / thymic hyperplasia | **Mandatory in all new MG** – 15% have thymoma | Thymoma = surgical resection. Hyperplasia may prompt thymectomy in young AChR+ patients. |
| **Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS)** | Decremental response (>10% drop in amplitude at low-frequency stimulation) | Objective evidence of neuromuscular transmission defect | **Positive confirms MG** – high specificity. False negatives possible if only ocular. |
| **Single-fibre EMG** | Increased jitter (variability in time between two muscle fibres) | Most sensitive test – even picks up ocular MG | **Highly sensitive >95%.** Can be positive in other NMJ disorders (LEMS, botulism) so not specific alone. |
| **Edrophonium test** (if done) | Rapid (<1 min) improvement in strength after IV edrophonium | Quick bedside confirmation (rarely used now) | **Improvement strongly suggests MG.** Risk of bradycardia – have atropine ready. |

**Single finding that confirms diagnosis:**  
A **positive anti-AChR antibody** in a patient with compatible clinical features.

**Single finding that rules out MG (almost):**  
A **normal single-fibre EMG** from a clinically weak muscle (e.g., frontalis, extensor digitorum) – but still 5% false negatives.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – for new diagnosis or crisis:**

- **Assess airway, breathing, circulation.** If respiratory distress (tachypnoea, accessory muscles, unable to count to 20 in one breath, or dropping O2 sats) → **call ICU** – myasthenic crisis.  
- **NIF (negative inspiratory force) < -20 cmH2O or forced vital capacity < 15 mL/kg** – immediate intubation indication.  
- **Stop any neuromuscular blocking agents (NMBs)** – e.g., vecuronium, rocuronium (prolonged effect in MG).  
- **Administer pyridostigmine** (if already on it) – but in crisis, IV neostigmine may be used first by anaesthetist (rare).  

**Ward (first 24-72 hours) – stabilisation and workup:**

- **Start pyridostigmine** 30-60 mg PO 3-4 times daily (max 120 mg/dose). CR if needed. *Why:* anticholinesterase increases ACh availability at synaptic cleft. Onset 30-60 min, duration 3-4 hours.  
- **Corticosteroids** – **prednisolone** 20-40 mg daily (or start 10 mg and taper up to avoid early exacerbation). *Why:* immunosuppression – turns off antibody production. **Watch for initial worsening** in first 2 weeks (steroid-induced exacerbation). Have rescue plan (IVIG/plasma exchange).  
- **Azathioprine** (1-2 mg/kg/day) or mycophenolate mofetil – as steroid-sparing. Takes 4-6 months to work.  
- **IVIG** (0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days) or **plasma exchange** (5-7 exchanges over 2 weeks) – for acute severe exacerbations or crisis.  
- **Thymectomy** – indicated if thymoma (any age) or if AChR+ generalised MG < 60 years (improves outcomes). Wait until stable, often after steroids are established.  

**Long-term:**

- **Maintenance immunosuppression** – prednisolone weaned to lowest effective dose (often 5-10 mg daily) plus azathioprine/mycophenolate.  
- **Pyridostigmine** as needed for symptoms.  
- **Rituximab** – for refractory MG (especially MuSK+).  
- **Avoid** – statins, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, beta-blockers (including eye drops for glaucoma), verapamil, magnesium.  

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do NOT give magnesium** – it blocks ACh release and can precipitate crisis.  
- **Do NOT use suxamethonium** for anaesthesia – metabolism is normal, but depolarising block can be prolonged if receptors are damaged? Actually, suxamethonium often *resistance* in MG? Wait: MG patients may be resistant to depolarising NMB (suxamethonium) and sensitive to non-depolarising (rocuronium). But safer to avoid both or use at reduced doses.  
- **Do NOT start high-dose prednisolone without planning for exacerbation** – start low and build.  
- **Do NOT forget to check thymoma** – missing a tumour.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Myasthenia Gravis | Lambert-Eaton (LEMS) | Botulism | Oculopharyngeal MD | ALS (Bulbar) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Pain** | None | None | None (blurred vision, but no pain) | None | None (but cramps) |
| **Exam** | Fatigable weakness, normal reflexes, no sensory loss | Proximal weakness *improves* with exercise, depressed/absent reflexes, autonomic (dry mouth, impotence) | Descending flaccid paralysis, dilated fixed pupils, bulbar, preserved EOM? In foodborne, symmetric, no fever | Slowly progressive ptosis, dysphagia, no fluctuation; family history | Spastic + flaccid, fasciculations, hyperreflexia, dysarthria, dysphagia with emotional lability |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | Anti-AChR/MuSK antibodies | Anti-VGCC antibodies (P/Q-type) | Stool/serum botulinum toxin | Genetic test (PABPN1 expansion) | EMG shows denervation, normal NMJ studies |
| **Key diff** | Fatigability, fluctuation, response to pyridostigmine | **Improvement with exercise**; associated with small cell lung cancer | **Acute onset**, history of contaminated food, autonomic signs (dry mouth, constipation) | **Family history**, gradual onset, no fluctuation | **UMN+LMN signs** (spasticity, fasciculations, brisk reflexes), relentless progression |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complication: Myasthenic crisis**

- **Presentation:** Rapid worsening of weakness, especially bulbar and respiratory. Patient can’t hold head up, can’t swallow saliva, becomes tachypnoeic, uses accessory muscles, eventually *silent chest* with desaturation.  
- **Key signs:** Staccato speech (short, soft phrases), nasal regurgitation, inability to lift head from pillow (*head drop* – very specific).  
- **What to do:** **Call ICU immediately.** Measure NIF and FVC. If FVC < 15 mL/kg or NIF < -20 cmH2O → intubate elective (before crash). Start IVIG or plasma exchange. **Do not give sedatives** – they may precipitate respiratory arrest.  
- **Other complications:**  
  - **Aspiration pneumonia** due to dysphagia  
  - **Thymoma** – risk of invasion, but thymic carcinoma is rarer.  
  - **Immunosuppression side effects**: osteoporosis, infections, diabetes.

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**  
- Patient with “long-standing ptosis and double vision” who eventually presents in crisis after a chest infection.  
- Elderly man with swallowing difficulty and weight loss, misdiagnosed as oesophageal cancer, but actually MG with bulbar onset.  
- Post-operative myasthenic crisis after a minor procedure (especially if non-depolarising NMBs were used).

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **ICE PACK test** – *Not a mnemonic, but a pearl:* Place an ice pack over a ptotic eyelid for 2 minutes. **If ptosis improves** → MG (cold slows acetylcholinesterase, increasing ACh at synapse).  
2. **MG’s Don’ts** – Remember: **Statins, Aminoglycosides, Macrolides, Fluoroquinolones, Beta-blockers, Magnesium**. Mnemonic: “**S.A.M. FlaM**” (Statins, Aminoglycosides, Macrolides, Fluoroquinolones, Magnesium). Beta-blockers added separately.  
3. **FOCAL weakness** – F: Fatigable, O: Ocular (ptosis/diplopia), C: Cranial/ bulbar, A: Antibodies (AChR/MuSK), L: LEMS mimics? Not perfect but helps recall features.

**Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

- **“Ice pack test”** is highly specific for MG ptosis – improvement suggests MG.  
- **“Peek sign”** – when you ask the patient to look up and close eyes tightly, ptosis worsens.  
- **“Lid twitch”** – after upgaze, when patient looks down, the eyelid twitches transiently (Cogan’s lid twitch).  
- **“Cogwheel” eye movements** – hypometric saccades with overshoot.  
- **Observe patient drinking water** – if they must pause to swallow or water dribbles from mouth, think bulbar MG.  
- **AChR antibodies can be negative in 15%**, especially ocular – if suspicion high, proceed to EMG.  
- **Thymoma on CT** – even if asymptomatic, resect because of malignant potential.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking MG for stroke – bilateral ptosis and diplopia do not fit single stroke lesion.  
- Giving edrophonium without atropine at bedside – bradycardia can be severe.  
- Prescribing antibiotics for a chest infection without checking safety – avoid macrolides and fluoroquinolones.  
- Starting steroids without first evaluating for thymoma – steroids can shrink hyperplasia but mask a thymoma.  

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

- **Respiratory failure** is the priority. Escalate if:
  - **FVC < 15 mL/kg** (or < 1 L in average adult)  
  - **NIF (negative inspiratory force) < -20 cmH2O**  
  - **SpO2 < 92% on room air** or dropping despite O2  
  - **Inability to clear secretions** (bulbar weakness)  
  - **Staccato speech** (can’t count to 20 in one breath)  
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (or ≥ 3 in any single parameter – desaturation, tachypnoea, tachycardia)  

- **Also escalate if:**  
  - **New onset of bulbar symptoms** (dysphagia to solids or liquids) – risk of aspiration  
  - **Rapidly worsening weakness over hours** – consider myasthenic crisis  
  - **Signs of cholinergic crisis** (if on pyridostigmine overdose) – muscle fasciculations, increased salivation, diarrhoea, bradycardia. Paradoxically, weakness may worsen. This is a **medical emergency** – stop pyridostigmine, call senior.

- **ICU admission criteria:**  
  - Any of the above respiratory signs  
  - Need for non-invasive ventilation (NIV) or intubation  
  - Severe dysphagia with inability to handle secretions (consider NG tube and NBM)

**Specific thresholds:**

| Parameter | Escalation Trigger |
|-----------|-------------------|
| FVC | < 15 mL/kg (adult ~ 1-1.2 L) |
| NIF | < -20 cmH2O (or > -20 indicates inadequate effort) |
| NEWS2 | ≥ 5 (or single parameter ≥ 3) |
| GCS | < 15 (if due to hypercapnia or sedation – avoid in MG) |
| Lactate | Not specific to MG, but rising lactate may indicate sepsis or ischemia |

**Remember:** In myasthenic crisis, intubate **early** before exhaustion. Prolonged bag-mask ventilation is difficult due to weak muscles. Have a low threshold for ICU.

---

*End of STUDY-MODE entry. You now have a dense, conceptual map of Myasthenia Gravis – pattern, mechanism, workup, management, and danger zones. Use it to build your own mental model.*
